e50000-7: A genuine toss-up: after correction the two calls finish a hundredth of an IMP apart, deep inside the noise. 1D is light — 11 HCP is below the card's floor — but two aces and a working king are exactly the values that outperform their raw count, and opening first claims the initiative and directs partner's attention. P is equally defensible: the hand is a flat 3-4-4-2 with no intermediates to speak of, partner will play an opener for 12+, and at both vulnerable an invitation accepted a point light is expensive. Almost half the deals push anyway. Principle: on borderline second-seat openings, prime honors argue for bidding and dead-flat shape argues for waiting — when the forces offset this evenly, temperament casts the deciding vote.
e50000-8: A toss-up with a huge push rate: on 91% of layouts both calls reach the same 2H contract and only the declarer differs. XX, the re-transfer, is the percentage call by a whisker: West's lead-directing double has marked the diamond attack, and forcing opener to declare keeps the 15-17 hand's honors protected, the lead running up to them instead of through them. Plain 2H puts the weak hand at the wheel and lays the strong hand down as dummy, letting West's marked diamond lead come through it — though it is simpler, and the redouble does hand the opponents an extra cheap round to find a diamond or spade fit. At 0.08 IMPs the margin is a coin flip. Principle: once the defense has advertised its best lead, use every systemic tool to make that lead run up to strength.
e50003-32: Too close to separate: 3H leads by 0.18 IMPs with a confidence interval twice that size, and two-thirds of layouts push. 3H is the systemic answer to Puppet Stayman — show the five-card suit, let the passed-hand partner raise with three, and collect the ruffing trick when the 5-3 fit plays better. 3NT is a deliberate off-card strike: this 3-5-2-3 twenty-count has AKQJ3 running opposite almost anything, aces in both minors, and tenaces begging for the opening lead to come around, so nine tricks in notrump usually match ten in hearts while the strong hand stays concealed. The lie costs only on the layouts where the 5-3 heart fit produces a decisive extra trick — rare enough here that the verdict cannot split the two. Principle: conceal a major only when the hand wants notrump opposite any fit.
e50006-19: A toss-up, though 1NT holds a modest half-IMP edge — real, but the intervals overlap. With 17 HCP and 4-4-3-2, 1NT is the card's bid: it limits the hand in one call, protects three side kings from the opening lead, and Stayman still finds either 4-4 major fit whenever partner can move. 1D is a deliberate off-card choice by pairs who fear the majors dying when a weak partner passes 1NT; its price is the rebid problem — over a 2C or 2D response nothing describes this strength and shape honestly, and partner reads the hand as unbalanced or outside 15-17. The numbers say the extra major-fit auctions are roughly paid for by the descriptive muddle, no more. Principle: with 15-17 balanced and no five-card suit, open 1NT and let system, not hope, find the fit.
e50006-27: 4S and 5D are statistically equivalent — a toss-up, pushing 93% of the time — while 4D is a genuine error costing about 2.3 IMPs. Facing partner's forcing 4C, this hand has exploded in value: AJT9 sitting over the weak-two bidder, six diamonds to the AQJ, a singleton heart. But 4D can be read as a minimum overcall and passed, stranding the partnership in a partscore precisely when the combined hands produce eleven or twelve tricks. 4S, the cue-bid, commits to a minor-suit game, shows the spade control and invites partner to evaluate slam; 5D announces the self-sufficient suit and reaches the same good contracts by a blunter route — the simulation cannot tell the two apart. Principle: when your hand improves after partner forces, remove the option to pass; the cheapest-sounding call is the only losing one.
e50007-23: A three-way toss-up: 4D noses out 3S by 0.02 IMPs, 3H trails by 0.21, and every gap sits inside the error bars. 4D sets the 4-4 diamond fit and advertises slam-suitable prime cards — AK75 of diamonds, no wasted heart honors — at the cost of driving past 3NT. 3S re-offers the five-card major; opposite a doubleton honor the 5-2 spade game can outscore the minor. 3H is the off-card probe: systemically it promises heart values, so partner bids 3NT with as little as Qx and the singleton is exposed at trick one — yet when partner holds real heart cards anyway, that same notrump game is often the winning spot, which is why the lie stays afloat. Principle: in a game-forcing fit auction the three-level calls are cheap descriptions — spend the honest ones before committing past 3NT.
e50007-31: Pass and 2S finish 0.02 IMPs apart — a pure toss-up — and the verdict's real teeth are in the rejected invitations. 2NT costs about 2.2 IMPs, losing outright 86% of the time: nine HCP built on two unsupported queens is no invitation opposite a 12-14 rebid. 2D, new-minor forcing, drops about 1.4 the same way while burning extra bidding space — even when it finds a 5-3 spade fit, the level is already too high. Between the sane calls the forces offset: 2S gains whenever the five-card suit outplays notrump, and loses when partner holds a small doubleton spade and 1NT was cruising; P keeps the notrump upside on the flat days. Principle: an invitation needs working cards, not just nine points — with soft queens, choose between quitting and the five-card haven, and do not invite.
e50011-12: Jacoby 2NT and the limit raise 3S are a toss-up — 0.14 IMPs apart with 44% pushes — while the lazy 2S costs about a full IMP. That underbid is the real mistake: four trumps and two prime aces are too much playing strength for a 6-10 raise, and partner duly passes out makeable games. The question is how hard to upgrade this 11-count. 3S is the by-the-card action for a dead-flat 4-3-3-3 and keeps a minimum opener out of thin games. 2NT deviates by a king from the 13+ game force, betting that aces, a fourth trump and a fitting queen outperform their raw count; it wins opposite shapely minimums and pays when partner goes slamming. Principle: upgrade for aces and a fourth trump, downgrade for 4-3-3-3 — when the two rules collide, either raise is defensible.
e50012-16: 1D wins, by a modest but real margin — about 0.6 IMPs over P and a full IMP over 1NT. Two aces and KJT5 of diamonds make this 12-count a routine opening: bid 1D, rebid 1NT over a major response to show 12-14 balanced, and every later partscore fight starts on your terms. P is timid: it hands East a free run in third seat and hides the hand's defensive and lead-directing values, and the simulation prices that silence at over half an IMP. 1NT is the costly lie — a full zone below the 15-17 card range — because partner's invitations, transfers and game raises all arrive three points light, occasionally stealing the board but more often buying a hopeless game. Principle: aces and intermediates make a 12-count an opening bid; they do not make it a strong notrump.
e50020-17: A toss-up at the top: 2D edges the unusual 2NT by 0.41 IMPs, inside coin-flip territory, while P costs nearly a full IMP. Silence is the mistake — with 5-5 in the minors, a singleton spade and nothing wasted, passing surrenders the auction despite ideal conditions for acting (nobody vulnerable). Between the bids the trade is a classic: 2NT shows both suits in one call and steals space, but it commits the side to the three level on 9 HCP and maps the hand for the play whenever the opponents buy it. 2D is a shade light by the card's soundness standard — licensed by the KQT84 texture — and keeps the auction lower, though the clubs may never be heard from. Principle: shapely 5-5 hands are bid on playing tricks, not HCP; choose your risk, but do not pass.
